story:
Henry Bishop works hard at a desk job he hates, under a boss who seems to hate him back. One morning Mr. Carn stomps to Henry’s station, shouts about a late report, and bellows, “I want that report on my desk before the end of the day!” When Carn storms off, Henry mutters, “Loudmouth bastard. I wish it were legal to kill people like him.” He knows it’s a bitter, ugly thought, but the way Carn yells makes Henry feel sick.

On weekends, Henry tries to forget his job by walking the beach and wandering the city. One Saturday he steps into a dusty curio shop. He picks up a strange, one-page book. The single line in English reads: “You don’t have to die.” Beneath it are symbols he doesn’t recognize. The shopkeeper appears. Henry asks why a book would say such a thing. “Not everyone has to die,” the man replies. He leads Henry to a back room, opens a drawer, and brings out a dagger. “What if I told you I can’t die?” Henry scoffs—until the man thrusts the blade into his own stomach, then calmly pulls it out. The wound seals before Henry’s eyes. “It’s not a trick,” the man says, and even makes Henry push the dagger in so Henry can’t deny what he sees.

From a small box, the shopkeeper lifts a vial. “Drink this, and you won’t die either.” Henry hesitates, then swallows it. “I don’t feel anything,” he says—just as the man suddenly plunges the knife into Henry. Henry gasps, pulls the dagger free, and watches his skin knit shut in seconds. Shaken, he drifts out of the shop in a daze. On the way home he buys a hunting knife. “Mr. Carn,” he mutters, “I bought you a present.”

On Monday Carn starts in on him again—this time about grammar in the Compton report. “One more blunder and you’re history,” Carn snarls. Henry says, “If you’ll stop yelling a moment, I want to show you a present,” takes out the knife, and drives it into Carn’s chest. He does not run. He waits for the police, is arrested, and pleads guilty over his public defender’s objection. State law mandates death, so he is sent to death row. In the visiting room, Henry asks his lawyer, George, “If the condemned can’t be executed—if it fails—does he have to be freed?” George is cautious. “In theory, if the state can’t carry out a lawful sentence, there are legal issues. But Henry, everyone can be executed.” “Not me,” Henry says. “Don’t file appeals.” He plans to prove his immortality in the execution chamber and walk out alive. It’s a reckless plan built on a rumor, but he clings to it.

Two weeks later George arrives with news: the state supreme court has abolished the death penalty. Henry’s sentence is converted to life without parole. Instead of walking free, he will live forever in a cell. “No,” Henry sobs. “I want to be executed.” Guards pull him away as he repeats, “I can’t spend eternity in prison. I can’t.”

Henry’s hatred led to a murder he can’t undo. The “gift” he grabbed in anger becomes a curse: immortality with no way out and no way back.
